title: Natural reader got me into writting again.

transcript.text: I wanted to say that Natural Reader got me into writing again.
Well, it didn't get me into writing. I'm not writing.
It got me excited about my future self writing later on when I'm not doing what I'm doing now.
Before I kind of got turned off to writing, well, Natural Reader, let me explain what that is.
Natural Reader is the program you can get for $30, which turns text into voice.

So if you're perusing the internet and you want to read some article, you just click play and then you can browse around the internet or do whatever you're doing and you can be listening to it at the same time.
Or you could just transfer it to a file and then put it on your MP3.
And I love it because I get to multitask.
It actually got me really excited about being a, I want to be a, I was a window washer last year, but I want to be a window washer again.
It seems like the perfect job for me because then I get to have a reason to listen to all this stuff I have, these MP3 files I have because I'm making an audio online encyclopedia.
And you can listen to that working.
It's kind of harder for me to listen to it when I'm like riding a bike or like lifting weights.
I kind of get a lot of the times dragged in to just listen to music.

But, but I do listen to it, um, the, the, uh, educational stuff, but, um, it got me really excited about, uh, about writing though too, because like I listened to the Ted conference a couple months ago and just, they were saying interesting stuff, but I had trouble listening to a lot of them.
And I actually, I didn't do it, but I almost, and it wasn't pleasurable for me to all listen to these people.
And I almost couldn't listen to them because these people like they're older people and they'd smack.
They had way too good of acoustics and you could hear like the smacking, which was grossing me out.
And then some people just don't have good voices.
Like one girl sounded like she was like whining, like on the edge of tears or something.
And, you know, some people just don't have good voices.
And I like, I like listening to, uh, the voice they have on natural reader is really good.
Um, one of them is, uh, Kate.
And, uh, when you're listening to natural reader, you're not listening to somebody, somebody and you're, so you're not getting anything clouded.
You're not getting any distractions of who they are.
You're just getting the raw data of what they're seeing, uh, which is cool.
Um, and, and it kind of puts everybody on a level playing ground too.

And you can also detect the differences between people's minds, I think better with natural reader than with them talking because with them talking, well, you know, I know a lot of people are going to disagree with me on that.
You know, I think it's maybe even common knowledge among like communication experts that 99% of communication is voice inflection and tone and all that shit.
But, but I mean, on the other side, I don't really agree that on the other side, what I care about is what somebody is saying.
I don't care how they're saying it.
I mean, if somebody wants to stress a word, they can just capitalize it.
And I think in natural reader, they, the person stresses it if they capitalize it, you know, or you just write it a certain way.
And I really liked that.
And, but anyways, the whole point of this video is natural reader really got me excited about writing myself.

Because I can write, and then I can listen to myself write, and I can tell, I can, I can like compare myself to other people on the same exact level playing ground, because it's the same person reading it.
And it also makes it easier to proofread.
I can really tell problems in syntax or spelling when you listen to it, even if you're not paying attention.
Um, and that's kind of one reason that I got turned off to writing is because I, I, I'd write something and then I'd read it again.
And I'd be like, what?
There'd always be problems, like grammatical problems.
And I don't know how it happened.
Like I, I thought I proofread it, but then it screwed up again.
But with natural reader, you can, you can fix it a lot quicker because you can just listen to it.
And then you'd be like, oh, here, it was messed up here.
And then you just go in and boom, fix it.

But I like to, I want to write just so that I can listen to it on natural reader, just so I can listen to myself to find out who, what kind of a writer I am, what kind of a communicator I am, what kind of ideas I have.
I can, and it's a lot less time consuming to listen to myself when I'm writing a lot than to have to read it again.
Cause I can put my own stuff on, up on, uh, my MP3 player and listen to it later.
So, uh, so, and I'm kind of hoping turning, I think, and I think it's the future.
Um, uh, I think a lot of people are going to be doing natural reader.
I don't know if it's the only program, but are going to be listening to, uh, audio more than writing.
Um, once people figure it out that, that you can, in fact, retain stuff if you listen to it, if it's interesting enough.
And even if it's not interesting, you can still retain it.
You know, your subconscious gets it and you get the gist of stuff.
And, uh, so many people do, and this is from another video, but so many people do jobs, labor jobs and stuff where they have plenty of time to listen to stuff.
And I think it'll catch on, you know, traffic standing in lines.
And so anybody who writes something is always, is naturally going to have an option to download the text.
I know in blog spot blog program, you can, you can already push the button and it reads it for you.
Um, so, um, so yeah, I want to, you know, if you want to get your ideas across, there's two ways to do it, writing it and speaking it.
But, but writing it actually, and, but speaking it's better, but writing it is actually, as far as I'm concerned, writing it and turning it into text is better than speaking it.
Um, because you, um, your ideas are a lot better thought out.
Um, and, and, uh, but you get the best of it because people can actually listen to it and do the multitasking thing with it.

So anyways, every like idea I do, every video I do for YouTube and any, and everything, I'm going to eventually have that in an article where I write it out, you know, well thought, write it all out and then have it turned into natural reader.
And then I can listen to it again and I can like compare myself with all the other people who I listened to, um, and, you know, see what my writing style is.
Uh, it's, you, you, I think I'll get a lot more insights about my writing style and, um, the way I express myself through a unbiased, um, audio rendition of, of what I wrote.
Um, so, um, and I'm glad I'm getting back into writing.
I thought I was like over with writing.

A lot of people don't like to write because you're not asked to write so much in school and people use it as punishment, you know, after school, write this a thousand times and people just don't like to write.
Um, and, uh, but with the whole use of the internet, of the, uh, um, uh, audio stuff, um, if people, anybody who wants to get their point out, I think, uh, we'll have to,
Write because then they can get it into audio.
Um, not to say that making videos and talking is definitely cool too.
